title: An Optimizer's Clear Winner Isn't Always Clearly Better
summary: Excel Solver picked Portfolio 1 over Portfolio 2 with a clean expected-return edge — but once you simulate 1,000 uncertain market scenarios, that edge nearly disappears.
takeaway: A point-estimate optimization can look decisive while the underlying real-world edge is closer to a coin flip.

sections.overview: While building a portfolio optimization model, Excel Solver selected Portfolio 1 (SPY, QQQ, VNQ, GLD, BND) over an alternative, more diversified Portfolio 2 based on expected monthly return alone. On paper, it looked like a clean win.
sections.problem: Point-estimate optimization treats expected returns as fixed numbers, which can make one portfolio look meaningfully better than another even when the actual difference in a given month could easily go either way. The question was whether Portfolio 1's edge was real or just an artifact of using a single average return per asset.
sections.approach: I ran a 1,000-scenario Monte Carlo simulation on both portfolios, treating returns as uncertain rather than fixed, and directly tracked how often Portfolio 1 actually outperformed Portfolio 2 across those scenarios, rather than just comparing their expected values.
methodology: Simulated 1,000 market scenarios using the historical mean and standard deviation of each asset's monthly returns.
Computed each portfolio's simulated return in every scenario, then compared average simulated return, volatility, and head-to-head win rate between Portfolio 1 and Portfolio 2.

results.summary: Portfolio 1's advantage held up directionally but shrank considerably once uncertainty was modeled explicitly, rather than assuming returns would land exactly at their historical averages.
points: Portfolio 1's expected monthly return (4.68%) was noticeably higher than Portfolio 2's (4.45%) in the point-estimate optimization.
Across 1,000 simulated scenarios, Portfolio 1 averaged 4.23% versus Portfolio 2's 4.15% — a much narrower gap.
Portfolio 1 outperformed Portfolio 2 in only 51.2% of simulated scenarios — barely better than a coin flip.

lessons: A single expected-return number can make a choice look obvious when the real-world margin is thin — simulation is what reveals that.
Both portfolios were legitimately viable; the 'optimal' one wasn't a dramatically safer or better choice, just a slightly favored one.

title: A Variable Can Be Significant Only Because of What Else Is in the Model
summary: In a credit default model, one variable (phone ownership) was statistically significant — until other correlated variables were removed, at which point it wasn't anymore.
takeaway: Statistical significance describes a variable's role in a specific model, not a fixed, standalone property of that variable.

sections.overview: While narrowing down a credit default model from 20 candidate variables to a final significant set, one variable — whether the applicant had a telephone — was significant in an earlier version of the model, but lost its significance later, after other variables were removed.
sections.problem: It's tempting to treat a variable's p-value as a fixed fact about how important that variable is. This case showed that a variable's apparent importance can actually be borrowed from other correlated variables sitting alongside it in the model.
sections.approach: After using best-subset selection to identify a strong candidate set of variables, I refit the model and removed variables that turned out not to be statistically significant, one round at a time, watching how each removal affected the p-values of everything still in the model.
methodology: Refit the logistic regression after each round of variable removal rather than dropping several variables blind and refitting once.
Tracked how individual p-values shifted specifically after removing variables like housing status and foreign-worker status, which plausibly correlate with phone ownership.

results.summary: The telephone variable's significance depended on other variables still being present in the model — once those correlated variables were dropped, telephone's p-value drifted from significant to right around the 0.10 range.
points: Telephone ownership was significant (p < 0.05) while variables like housing and foreign-worker status were still in the model.
After removing those variables for being individually insignificant, telephone's p-value rose to roughly 0.10 and it was dropped in the next round.

lessons: Significance is contextual — it reflects a variable's contribution given everything else in the model, not an absolute property of the variable itself.
Refitting after every single removal, instead of dropping a batch of variables at once, is what actually caught this — a good reminder to iterate rather than filter in one big pass.

title: Vector Search Finds Similar Text, Not Connected Facts
summary: Building a knowledge-graph chatbot made the real limitation of plain vector RAG obvious: it can find text that sounds related, but it can't trace how two things are actually connected.
takeaway: Semantic similarity tells you two things are related in meaning; only an explicit graph structure tells you how.

sections.overview: Building a Neo4j-based knowledge graph chatbot on top of a COVID-19 dataset surfaced a distinction that's easy to gloss over when using vector RAG: similarity and connection are not the same thing.
sections.problem: Standard vector RAG retrieves chunks of text that are semantically close to a query, but 'semantically close' isn't the same as 'causally or structurally connected.' Questions that require tracing a chain — like how SARS-CoV-2 leads to pneumonia, which leads to hospitalization — expose that gap directly.
sections.approach: I built a hybrid pipeline where vector similarity is used only for the narrow task of matching a query to relevant entities, while the actual reasoning happens by traversing explicit, typed relationships in a knowledge graph — including 2-hop paths for multi-step chains.
methodology: Retrieved entities via a Neo4j vector index over entity embeddings, combined with keyword matching.

Explicitly traversed 1-hop facts and 2-hop paths from those entities, then used an LLM reranking step to select the evidence most relevant to the query before generating an answer grounded only in that evidence.

results.summary: The graph-traversal step is what actually answers 'how' and 'why' questions well — vector search alone only gets you to related entities, not the reasoning chain between them.
points: 2-hop path retrieval surfaced multi-step chains (e.g., cause → effect → downstream consequence) that a single similarity lookup wouldn't reveal.
Every generated answer is returned alongside the specific facts or paths used, making the reasoning behind the answer auditable rather than a black-box similarity score.

lessons: Vector and graph retrieval aren't competitors — the strongest design used vector similarity for entity matching and graph structure for the actual reasoning.
Explainability isn't a side benefit of graph-based retrieval — it comes directly from the evidence being explicit, typed relationships instead of similarity scores.
